skill.name: vet
skill.description: Run the verify-everything vet tool after code changes to review git diffs and optional AI session history.
skill.summary: Run vet reviews after each logical code change.
skill.author: imbue-ai

security_audit.summary: Static weak-cryptography findings are false positives; the cited lines contain descriptions, command examples, or argument parsing, not cryptographic operations. The confirmed risks are intentional external command execution and reading local assistant session history from hidden directories, which are legitimate for this skill but require user awareness.

medium_findings.title: External Review Tool Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to install and run the verify-everything vet command, including history-loader commands. This is expected behavior for the skill, but it executes local commands and depends on an external package.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and central to the skill workflow. The risk is medium because the commands are documented review operations, not hidden or obfuscated execution.

medium_findings.title: Local Conversation History Access
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents to read Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode session files from hidden local directories and pass the extracted history to vet. This can expose sensitive prompts, code, or credentials contained in prior conversation history.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths and loader scripts directly read local assistant history files. The behavior is intentional and useful, but the privacy impact is clear.

low_findings.title: Unvalidated OpenCode Session Identifier
low_findings.description: The OpenCode export script uses the provided session identifier to build local paths without validating that it matches the expected ses_ format. This could read unexpected local JSON files if an untrusted session identifier is supplied.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script does not validate the session-id value before path construction. Exploitability appears limited because the script reads local JSON files and requires local command invocation.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Cryptography Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The reported weak-cryptography locations do not contain cryptographic operations. They are descriptions, argument parser declarations, or documentation text, so these scanner hits are false positives.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no hashing, encryption, cipher selection, or cryptographic API use at the cited locations. The static pattern appears to match unrelated text.

low_findings.title: Local Session Discovery Commands
low_findings.description: The documentation recommends grep-based discovery of session files in OpenCode storage. This is local reconnaissance for a stated workflow, but it can reveal where conversation records are stored.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The local discovery behavior is explicitly documented and has a legitimate purpose. It remains a low privacy concern because it searches assistant storage directories.

dangerous_patterns.title: History Loader Executes Local Python Scripts
dangerous_patterns.description: The standard usage examples pass Python history-loader commands to vet. This pattern executes local scripts that extract assistant conversation data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is visible in the documented usage examples. It is not malicious by itself, but it combines command execution with sensitive local file access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Assistant History Is Printed for Downstream Processing
dangerous_patterns.description: The export scripts parse local session files and print normalized user and assistant messages. This can move sensitive conversation content into another tool process.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The scripts clearly print extracted conversation text in normalized message objects. The risk depends on how vet stores or transmits that downstream data.

content.user_title: Run Vet Reviews After Code Changes
content.value_statement: AI coding sessions can miss regressions after small changes. This skill runs vet against diffs and optional session history to catch issues early.
actual_capabilities: Runs the vet command after logical code changes.
Reviews git diffs from a selected base commit.
Uses optional conversation history loaders for Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode.
Helps compare requested work against implemented changes.
Documents common vet options for output format, model choice, and confidence threshold.
limitations: Requires the verify-everything package to be installed locally.
Does not replace unit tests, integration tests, or manual review.
May include changes from other sessions when reviewing the full git diff.
Conversation history loading can expose sensitive local prompts or code.

use_cases.title: Check AI-Assisted Changes
use_cases.description: Run vet after each small implementation step to identify missed requirements or regressions.

use_cases.title: Validate Agent Output
use_cases.description: Use session history with git diff review to compare an agent request with the final code changes.

use_cases.title: Prepare Safer Reviews
use_cases.description: Generate a focused pre-review pass before asking a teammate to inspect the code.

prompt_templates.title: Run Basic Vet
prompt_templates.prompt: Run vet for the latest code change and summarize any issues it reports.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check after a small edit

prompt_templates.title: Review With Goal
prompt_templates.prompt: Run vet with this goal: confirm the implementation matches the requested behavior and does not introduce regressions.
prompt_templates.scenario: Focused review after completing a task

prompt_templates.title: Use Session History
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the current session history, run vet with the proper history loader, and explain only actionable findings.
prompt_templates.scenario: Review that compares conversation intent with code changes

prompt_templates.title: Tune Review Output
prompt_templates.prompt: Run vet from the selected base commit with a higher confidence threshold and provide a concise triage plan.

output_examples.input: Run vet after adding a validation rule.
output_examples.output: Vet reviewed the diff and found one likely issue: the new validation path lacks a test for empty input.

output_examples.input: Use session history to confirm the feature matches the request.
output_examples.output: Vet compared the conversation with the diff and reported that the implementation covers the main flow but not the requested error state.
Vet completed without actionable findings. Tests should still be run before merging.

best_practices: Run vet after each logical change instead of waiting for a large batch.
Review sensitive session history before passing it to external tools.
Pair vet findings with tests and direct code inspection.
anti_patterns: Do not treat vet as a replacement for the test suite.
Do not pass unrelated or sensitive session history without user consent.
Do not ignore findings solely because the change came from another session.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It guides an agent to run the verify-everything vet tool after code changes.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The instructions include usage paths for Claude, Codex, Claude Code, and OpenCode session history.

faq.question: Does it require installation?
faq.answer: Yes. The local verify-everything package must be installed before the vet command can run.

faq.question: Can it read conversation history?
faq.answer: Yes. Optional loader scripts read local assistant session files to provide context for review.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with warnings because it intentionally runs commands and reads local history files.

faq.question: Does vet replace tests?
faq.answer: No. The skill states that vet is not a replacement for running tests.
